title: ABC Journalists Walk Out in Historic First Strike in Two Decades
description: Australian Broadcasting Corporation staff stage 24-hour walkout over below-inflation pay offers, marking the public broadcaster's first industrial action since 2005.
lede: Journalists at the Australian Broadcasting Corporation have launched their first strike in 20 years, walking off the job for 24 hours in a dispute over pay that has laid bare tensions between public broadcasters and the governments that fund them. The walkout forced ABC to switch to BBC World Service programming across its radio networks.

sections.heading: The Walkout
paragraphs: ABC staff across the country downed tools at midnight on Tuesday, leaving newsrooms empty and studios silent for the first time since a brief walkout in 2005. The strike affects television, radio, and digital operations, with the Media Entertainment and Arts Alliance union representing more than 1,800 ABC employees.

The immediate trigger was management's latest pay offer of 3.5% over two years — well below Australia's current inflation rate of 4.1%. Workers say their real wages have been eroding for years under successive budget freezes imposed by both Labor and Liberal governments.

ABC management deployed pre-recorded content and BBC World Service broadcasts to fill the gaps, but flagship programs including 7:30, News Breakfast, and Radio National's AM were replaced with reruns or simulcasts.

sections.heading: Why Now
paragraphs: The strike comes after 18 months of failed negotiations. ABC employees point to a growing gap between executive compensation — managing director David Anderson earned $1.07 million in 2025 — and rank-and-file pay that has fallen 8% in real terms since 2020.

Union delegates say the dispute is about more than money. Staff are pushing back against plans to expand AI-generated content, reduce regional newsroom headcounts, and restructure the organization in ways they say will hollow out Australian public journalism.

sections.heading: The Political Dimension
paragraphs: The Albanese government has tried to distance itself from the dispute, calling it a matter between ABC management and its workforce. But critics note that federal funding decisions directly shape what ABC can offer its staff.

The Coalition opposition seized on the strike to renew calls for ABC reform, with shadow communications minister David Coleman arguing the broadcaster needs 'structural change, not just more money.' Media analysts say the strike exposes a fundamental tension in public broadcasting models worldwide.

sections.heading: Public Broadcasting Under Pressure
paragraphs: ABC's walkout mirrors labor unrest at public broadcasters globally. The BBC faced its own strikes in 2023, while Canada's CBC has shed hundreds of positions. In each case, the pattern is the same: flat government funding meets rising costs and digital transformation demands.

Australian media unions warn that if ABC cannot retain experienced journalists, the quality gap between public and commercial news will narrow — undermining the rationale for public funding altogether.

sections.heading: What Happens Next
paragraphs: The 24-hour action ended at midnight Wednesday, with staff returning to their desks for the morning news cycle. But union leaders say this is just the beginning if management doesn't return with a serious offer.

The MEAA has flagged the possibility of rolling stoppages and targeted blackouts of high-profile programs if talks remain stalled. Both sides are due back at the negotiating table next week, with federal mediation now on the cards.

faqs.q: Why are ABC journalists on strike?
faqs.a: ABC staff walked out for 24 hours over a pay offer of 3.5% over two years, which falls below Australia's inflation rate. They also oppose plans to expand AI content and reduce regional newsrooms.

faqs.q: When was the last ABC strike?
faqs.a: The last ABC strike was in 2005, making this walkout the first industrial action at Australia's national broadcaster in 20 years.

faqs.q: How did ABC fill programming during the strike?
faqs.a: ABC switched to BBC World Service broadcasts on radio and aired pre-recorded content on television, replacing flagship programs like News Breakfast and 7:30.

faqs.q: How many ABC employees went on strike?
faqs.a: More than 1,800 ABC employees represented by the Media Entertainment and Arts Alliance union participated in the 24-hour walkout.

faqs.q: Will there be more ABC strikes?
faqs.a: Union leaders have warned of rolling stoppages and targeted program blackouts if management doesn't improve its pay offer in upcoming negotiations.
